pattern: Process erasures in reverse and add bricks back with union-find: each restored brick that reconnects a region to the top tells you how many fell when it was removed forward.
hints: Stability means connectivity to the top row, so think in terms of connected components anchored to a virtual top node.
Running forward is hard because removals cascade; instead remove every hit first, then add the bricks back in reverse order.

Connect the virtual top node to every surviving top-row brick, then for each reversed hit place the brick and union it with stable neighbours; the newly stabilised count minus one (the placed brick itself) is the answer for that hit.

edge_inputs.description: removing the support drops the two bricks above it
both hits leave the rest stable, nothing falls
hit on an empty cell drops nothing
hitting a top-row brick itself is not counted as falling
single column, removing the top brick drops the whole stack below
repeated hits on already-empty positions after the first
a brick stays up because a second path to the top survives

stress.description: small random grid with a handful of hits so the O(hits * grid) brute oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, 6)
    n = rng.randint(1, 6)
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < 0.6 else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    cells = [(r, c) for r in range(m) for c in range(n)]
    rng.shuffle(cells)
    k = rng.randint(1, min(size, len(cells)))
    hits = [[r, c] for (r, c) in cells[:k]]
    return [grid, hits]
stress.note: grid and hit count kept tiny because the brute oracle recomputes stability per hit
